2: Intensive agricultural production is related to a number of envi- ronmental problems (European Environmental Agency, 2006; Stoate et al., 2001; Tilman et al., 2002). High use of external inputs leads to adverse environmental impacts like demand for fossil

energy resources, phosphorus or potassium, increase in global warming potential, loss of biodiversity, degradation of soil quality (e.g. by erosion, compaction or loss of organic matter) and pollu- tion of water, soil and air. Low-input or extensive agriculture is considered a way of solv- ing many of the problems of intensive agriculture (Biala et al., 2007). Low-input farming systems are defined as farming systems that "seek to optimise the management and use of internal produc- tion inputs (i.e. on farm resources) and to minimise the use of external production inputs such as purchased fertiliser and

pesticides, wherever and whenever feasible and practical, to lower production costs, to avoid pollution of surface and ground water, to reduce pesticide residues in food, to reduce a farmer's overall risk, and to increase both short- long-term farm profitability" (Parr et al., 1990). In this paper intensity is defined as the amount of input (e.g. kg nitrogen or kg pesticide active matter) or the fre- quency of intervention per area and time unit. Low-input farming system and extensive farming system are used as synonyms.

One way to develop low-input farming was chosen by the or- ganic farming movement. The impacts of Swiss organic farming on the environment as compared to integrated production were dealt with in a related paper (Nemecek et al., 2011). The main envi- ronmental advantages of organic farming were found to be better resource conservation (fossil fuels and mineral resources), higher biodiversity potential and lower ecotoxicity potential, while its main drawbacks are comparatively low yields and relatively high nutrient losses per yield unit (Nemecek et al., 2011).

Another option to conversion from conventional or integrated production to organic farming is optimisation of the management within a given farming system, with the goal to reduce environ- mental impacts. Previous studies (Mouron et al., 2006; Rossier and Gaillard, 2001) have revealed considerable differences within a given farming system. The environmental performance of inte- grated production, for example, can be improved in many cases by reducing inputs. The question arising is whether such a reduc- tion of intensity really results in reduced environmental impacts.

On the one hand, several studies show advantages of extensive over intensive farming systems (Bailey et al., 2003; Casey and Holden, 2006; Haas et al., 2001; Moerschner et al., 1997; Oosting and de Boer, 2001). On the other hand, some environmental im- pacts of extensive systems may be higher than those of intensive systems (Basset-Mens and Van Der Werf, 2005; Brentrup et al., 2004; Gaillard and Nemecek, 2002; Hayashi, 2005; Taube et al., 2006). This can be due to a reduction of yields in extensive sys- items, a change in the inputs applied (e.g. manure VS. mineral fertil- iser) or a change in techniques (e.g. mechanical weeding VS. herbicide application or ploughing VS. no-till agriculture). The resulting environmental impact of an extensive system is in fact the consequence of complex processes that affect yield on the one hand and resource consumption and emissions on the other hand and requires comprehensive analysis. The question arising is whether and under which conditions extensive farming systems are really preferable from an environmental point of view.

In order to design a low-input system we can either opt for overall extensification or partial extensification. The purpose of this paper is to investigate these extensification options for the integrated production as mentioned in the following.

In overall extensification the whole farming system is extensified in a balanced manner, taking care to keep a good equilibrium be- tween the different inputs and interventions. This approach is investigated for two case studies in this paper: (a) extensification of arable cropping systems and (b) extensification of grassland management.

In partial extensification only certain inputs and interventions are reduced, while keeping the others at a similar level. We investigate three options: (a) reduced fertiliser application (including also the change of the type of fertiliser from mineral to organic as a special form of extensification), (b) reduced plant protection and (c) reduced soil cultivation (no-till agriculture). These three options represent topics of environmental concern as presented in Nemecek et al. (2011), namely nutrient manage- ment, pollutant management and resource management. A fourth axis would be irrigation (more specifically its reduction or avoidance). However, as irrigation is not a common practice in Swiss arable and forage production systems, it is not investi- gated in this paper.

The paper is structured according to this logic. First the overall extensification option is studied by arable cropping systems and forage production. Then, the three options for partial extensifica- tion are investigated, namely the reduction of fertilisation, plant protection and soil cultivation intensity.

This approach is investigated for two case studies in this paper: (a) extensification of arable cropping systems and (b) extensification of grassland management.

In partial extensification only certain inputs and interventions are reduced, while keeping the others at a similar level. We investigate three options: (a) reduced fertiliser application (including also the change of the type of fertiliser from mineral to organic as a special form of extensification), (b) reduced plant protection and (c) reduced soil cultivation (no-till agriculture). These three options represent topics of environmental concern as presented in Nemecek et al. (2011), namely nutrient manage- ment, pollutant management and resource management. A fourth axis would be irrigation (more specifically its reduction or avoidance). However, as irrigation is not a common practice in Swiss arable and forage production systems, it is not investi- gated in this paper.

The paper is structured according to this logic. First the overall extensification option is studied by arable cropping systems and forage production. Then, the three options for partial extensifica- tion are investigated, namely the reduction of fertilisation, plant protection and soil cultivation intensity.
7: Intensive agricultural production is related to a number of envi- ronmental problems (European Environmental Agency, 2006; Stoate et al., 2001; Tilman et al., 2002). High use of external inputs leads to adverse environmental impacts like demand for fossil

energy resources, phosphorus or potassium, increase in global warming potential, loss of biodiversity, degradation of soil quality (e.g. by erosion, compaction or loss of organic matter) and pollu- tion of water, soil and air. Low-input or extensive agriculture is considered a way of solv- ing many of the problems of intensive agriculture (Biala et al., 2007). Low-input farming systems are defined as farming systems that "seek to optimise the management and use of internal produc- tion inputs (i.e. on farm resources) and to minimise the use of external production inputs such as purchased fertiliser and

pesticides, wherever and whenever feasible and practical, to lower production costs, to avoid pollution of surface and ground water, to reduce pesticide residues in food, to reduce a farmer's overall risk, and to increase both short- long-term farm profitability" (Parr et al., 1990). In this paper intensity is defined as the amount of input (e.g. kg nitrogen or kg pesticide active matter) or the fre- quency of intervention per area and time unit. Low-input farming system and extensive farming system are used as synonyms.

One way to develop low-input farming was chosen by the or- ganic farming movement. The impacts of Swiss organic farming on the environment as compared to integrated production were dealt with in a related paper (Nemecek et al., 2011). The main envi- ronmental advantages of organic farming were found to be better resource conservation (fossil fuels and mineral resources), higher biodiversity potential and lower ecotoxicity potential, while its main drawbacks are comparatively low yields and relatively high nutrient losses per yield unit (Nemecek et al., 2011).

Another option to conversion from conventional or integrated production to organic farming is optimisation of the management within a given farming system, with the goal to reduce environ- mental impacts. Previous studies (Mouron et al., 2006; Rossier and Gaillard, 2001) have revealed considerable differences within a given farming system. The environmental performance of inte- grated production, for example, can be improved in many cases by reducing inputs. The question arising is whether such a reduc- tion of intensity really results in reduced environmental impacts.

On the one hand, several studies show advantages of extensive over intensive farming systems (Bailey et al., 2003; Casey and Holden, 2006; Haas et al., 2001; Moerschner et al., 1997; Oosting and de Boer, 2001). On the other hand, some environmental im- pacts of extensive systems may be higher than those of intensive systems (Basset-Mens and Van Der Werf, 2005; Brentrup et al., 2004; Gaillard and Nemecek, 2002; Hayashi, 2005; Taube et al., 2006). This can be due to a reduction of yields in extensive sys- items, a change in the inputs applied (e.g. manure VS. mineral fertil- iser) or a change in techniques (e.g. mechanical weeding VS. herbicide application or ploughing VS. no-till agriculture). The resulting environmental impact of an extensive system is in fact the consequence of complex processes that affect yield on the one hand and resource consumption and emissions on the other hand and requires comprehensive analysis. The question arising is whether and under which conditions extensive farming systems are really preferable from an environmental point of view.

Then, the three options for partial extensifica- tion are investigated, namely the reduction of fertilisation, plant protection and soil cultivation intensity.
9: The DOC experiment compared bio-dynamic (D), bio-organic (O) and conventional/integrated (C) farming systems. These farming systems differ mainly in the form and amount of fertilisers applied and in plant protection. The experiment encompasses three farm- ing systems and three fertiliser levels (normal fertiliser application level according to current practice (level 2), half (level and no fertiliser application (level 0)). Organic or mineral fertilisers or a mixture of both fertiliser forms are applied in the farming systems. The analysis covers the second and third crop rotation period (14 years, 1985-1998). The following crops were grown in the periods under consideration: potatoes, winter wheat, beetroot, winter barley and grass-clover ley (2 or 3 years). In addition, a grass-clover mixture and green manure were sown as catch crops.

The farming system experiment at Burgrain in Central Switzer- land tests the practicability of integrated and low-input farming systems. Until the year 2000 two crop rotations of six years were managed on six experimental plots each: one crop rotation was geared mainly to cash crop production (Cash-CR) and one mainly to feed production (Feed-CR). Three farming systems have been compared in the experiment since 1997: IP intensive (IPint), IP extensive (IPext) organic farming (ORG). The following crops were included in the two crop rotations (catch crops are written in parentheses): potato - (green manure) - winter wheat (grass- clover ley) - grain maize - spring barley - grass-clover ley (two years) for the Cash-CR, and silage maize - spring - winter bar- ley - grass-clover ley (three years) for the Feed-CR. We analysed the second crop rotation period 1997-2002 for the Cash-CR and 1997-2000 for the Feed-CR, which was stopped after 2000.
A more detailed description of the two experiments can be found in Nemecek et al. (2011).

The Oberacker no-till experiment in Zollikofen was started in 1994 with the goal of comparing conventional agriculture with the use of the plough (system PL) and a no-till cropping system (system NT) (Chervet et al., 2005). The experiment is performed on a brown soil with 15% clay and 3% humus. In this study we ana- lysed the 6 years from 1999 to 2005, since the first years of the experiment correspond to a transition phase. The following crops were grown in this period: winter wheat - silage maize - winter barley - sugar beet - winter wheat protein peas. Table 3 summa- rises the key production data (see also Schaller et al., 2006). The NT system achieved 4% higher yields than PL, with significantly less machinery interventions, a higher use of N fertiliser and a higher input of pesticide active ingredients, caused by the applications of glyphosate.

12: The arable area in Switzerland was about 407,000 ha in 2008, whereof 280,000 ha were arable crops and 127,000 ha temporary meadows (BLW, 2008). The definition of the production invento- ries for arable crops could not be based on a single data source, since no such comprehensive and up to date dataset was available for Switzerland. The inventories were therefore modelled using several data sources (see Nemecek et al., 2005b).

The weighted averages 1996-2003 from the Swiss Farm Accoun- tancy Data Network (FADN) were used for the yields (Hausheer Schnider and Meier, 2004). The FADN monitors data from about 3500 farms. The work processes, quantities of seed and the water content at harvest were taken from LBL et al. (2000). The dates for sowing, harvest and fertiliser application were based on the work budget by Näf (1996) and adapted by an accompanying expert group for factors not taken into account by Näf (1996), like produc- tion region. The figures for quantities of pesticides applied were taken from the pilot farm network of BLW et al. (1998) for the years 1994-1996. The quantities of nitrogen fertiliser and their split among the different applications were calculated in line with the fertiliser use recommendations (Walther et al., 2001). Corrections from this source for yield, catch crops, hoeing were included in the calculation. The types of mineral fertiliser were calculated according to the Swiss import statistics (Rossier, 2000); the quanti- ties of slurry and manure were taken from BLW et al. (1998).

The inventories for the different crops were adjusted for their internal consistency and checked by a group of experts of the Swiss Federal Agricultural Research Stations. The main production data are presented in Table 1; the full data are given by Nemecek et al. (2005b).

15: Swiss grassland systems are mainly used for feeding cattle. The 740,000 ha of grassland (without summer pastures) cover around two thirds of the agricultural area and grassland-based animal pro- duction systems deliver 35% of the agricultural gross output.

The forage yields were estimated according to Dietl (1986), who reports yields for average conditions in Switzerland over a range of pedo-climatic conditions. The losses of fodder on the field and dur- ing conservation originate from various Swiss studies (Frick and Ammann, 1999, 2000; Jakob and Van Caenegem, 1993; LBL et al., 2000). Fodder quality (energy content) stems from RAP (1999). Fertiliser application was calculated according to the fertiliser use recommendations for mixed grass-legumes swards (Walther et al., 2001). Since most Swiss farms with grassland keep animals and are strongly limited by the Swiss agri-environmental scheme in the amount of N and P mineral fertiliser they can purchase, we assumed that the fertiliser needs of the hay meadows are cov- ered by applications of farmyard manure (liquid manure for inten- sive and fairly intensive, solid manure for low intensive and no fertiliser for extensive management, Table 2). The widespread weed Rumex obtusifolius L. was controlled by 0.5 kg/ha Asulam in intensive and fairly intensive management (assuming a density

19: The life cycle assessment methodology was presented in detail in Nemecek et al. (2011). A brief summary is given in the following.

The considered part of the life cycle is cradle-to-gate. This means that all inputs and processes for the plant production are in- cluded as well as all the upstream processes necessary to produce these inputs. In particular the following items were considered: infrastructure (manufacturing and maintenance of machinery, buildings and equipments), operation of the machines (including fuel delivery and combustion), fertilisers manufacturing and appli- cation, pesticide production and application, field operations (soil tillage, seedbed preparation, sowing, fertilisation, plant protection, harvest, stubble cultivation, transport to the farm and grain dry- ing), and direct field and farm emissions.

For arable crops and crop rotation analyses we calculate for a dried and graded product at the farm gate; for the analysis of for- age production systems the reference is a conserved product after storage, delivered to the manger, since forage products are mostly used on the farm.

stuff inputs and emissions from animal husbandry, however, is fully attributed to the animal production system and therefore out- side the system boundary of plant production. For permanent crops (permanent grassland in this study) we consider one calen- dar year from January to December. For arable crops and the anal- ysis of crop rotations analysis starts after the harvest of the preceding crop and ends with the harvest of the crop analysed.

As agriculture is multifunctional, three functions and functional units are used: (i) the land management function, measured by cul- tivated hectare per year, (ii) the financial function expressed as a currency unit and (iii) the productive function described by physical units (kg dry matter yield, MJ net energy for lactation).

The life cycle inventories are taken from the ecoinvent database version 1.2 (corrected version of ecoinvent data v1.1; (Frischknecht et al., 2004b)). The direct field emissions were calculated by mod- els namely for ammonia (NH3, Menzi et al., 1997), nitrous oxide (N20, Schmid et al., 2000), phosphorus (Prasuhn, 2006), nitrate (NO, Richner et al., 2006) and heavy metal emissions (Freiermuth, 2006).

The following life cycle impact assessment methods were used: non-renewable energy resources (Frischknecht et al., 2004a), glo- bal warming potential over 100 years (IPCC, 2001), ozone forma- tion potential (so-called "summer smog" according to the EDIP97 method, Hauschild and Wenzel, 1998), eutrophication potential (impact of the losses of N and P to aquatic and terrestrial ecosys- items, according to the EDIP97 method, Hauschild and Wenzel, 1998), acidification potential (impact of acidifying substances re- leased into ecosystems, according to the EDIP97 method, Haus- child and Wenzel, 1998), terrestrial ecotoxicity potential and aquatic ecotoxicity potential (according to the EDIP97 method, Hauschild and Wenzel, 1998), human toxicity potential (according to the CML01 method, Guinée et al., 2001), biodiversity (Jeanneret et al., 2006), and soil quality (Oberholzer et al., 2006).

25: When assessing crops with different production intensities we need to evaluate significance and relevance of the differences, since LCA calculations are subject to various sources of uncertainty (see Nemecek et al., 2005b). A full analysis of statistical significance is

not feasible, because the uncertainty of many of the parameters is unknown. Consequently the differences between two crops were assessed by classes (see Table 4), based on the statistical variance of environmental impact indicators from a cropping system exper- iment and expert knowledge (see Nemecek et al., 2005b).

To assess the significance of the differences between the farm- ing systems, an analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed for the DOC, "Burgrain" and "Oberacker" experiments. In the first two experiments with more than two treatments, the significance between pairs of treatments was analysed by the Newman-Keuls multiple comparison test (for more details see Nemecek et al. (2011)).
26: 3.1. Reducing overall management intensity in arable cropping systems

The extensive integrated management (IPext) in the "Burgrain" experiment led to a significant reduction of between 12% and 71% in most environmental impacts per hectare and year as com- pared to intensive management (Table 5(a)). This confirms the finding that a reduction in inputs and management intensity re- duces area-related environmental impacts in almost all cases. The most notable reduction, of 71%, was in the terrestrial ecotoxic- ity potential. The main reasons for the moderated impacts were lower inputs of fertilisers in general and of mineral fertilisers in particular, and lower plant protection intensity. The results for soil quality were quite similar for these two systems, with a slight trend in favour of the IPint system, due to the quantity and applica- tion mode of manure: IP received more liquid and solid manure than ext. The high amount of easily degradable organic matter fa- vours microbial biomass and activity as well as earthworm popu- lations. Furthermore, in IPin all liquid manure was applied on grassland, whereas 23% on average were given to the arable crops in (to replace part of the mineral nitrogen for these crops). These applications are considered potentially harmful to earth- worms (Oberholzer et al., 2006). The biodiversity potential was slightly increased in ext mainly as a consequence of the replace- ment of unselective herbicides in IPint by mechanical weeding and selective herbicides in IPext.

Reduced impacts also resulted per kg of dry matter produced (Table 5(b)), although the differences were smaller and less signif- icant. Production conditions are favourable on this experimental site, with a soil rich in nutrients which allows the production of relatively high yields with a low nutrient input, leading to a yield loss of only 7% in IPext.

The most notable reduction, of 71%, was in the terrestrial ecotoxic- ity potential. The main reasons for the moderated impacts were lower inputs of fertilisers in general and of mineral fertilisers in particular, and lower plant protection intensity. The results for soil quality were quite similar for these two systems, with a slight trend in favour of the IPint system, due to the quantity and applica- tion mode of manure: IP received more liquid and solid manure than ext. The high amount of easily degradable organic matter fa- vours microbial biomass and activity as well as earthworm popu- lations. Furthermore, in IPin all liquid manure was applied on grassland, whereas 23% on average were given to the arable crops in (to replace part of the mineral nitrogen for these crops). These applications are considered potentially harmful to earth- worms (Oberholzer et al., 2006). The biodiversity potential was slightly increased in ext mainly as a consequence of the replace- ment of unselective herbicides in IPint by mechanical weeding and selective herbicides in IPext.

Reduced impacts also resulted per kg of dry matter produced (Table 5(b)), although the differences were smaller and less signif- icant. Production conditions are favourable on this experimental site, with a soil rich in nutrients which allows the production of relatively high yields with a low nutrient input, leading to a yield loss of only 7% in IPext.
28: 3.1. Reducing overall management intensity in arable cropping systems

The extensive integrated management (IPext) in the "Burgrain" experiment led to a significant reduction of between 12% and 71% in most environmental impacts per hectare and year as com- pared to intensive management (Table 5(a)). This confirms the finding that a reduction in inputs and management intensity re- duces area-related environmental impacts in almost all cases. The most notable reduction, of 71%, was in the terrestrial ecotoxic- ity potential. The main reasons for the moderated impacts were lower inputs of fertilisers in general and of mineral fertilisers in particular, and lower plant protection intensity. The results for soil quality were quite similar for these two systems, with a slight trend in favour of the IPint system, due to the quantity and applica- tion mode of manure: IP received more liquid and solid manure than ext. The high amount of easily degradable organic matter fa- vours microbial biomass and activity as well as earthworm popu- lations. Furthermore, in IPin all liquid manure was applied on grassland, whereas 23% on average were given to the arable crops in (to replace part of the mineral nitrogen for these crops). These applications are considered potentially harmful to earth- worms (Oberholzer et al., 2006). The biodiversity potential was slightly increased in ext mainly as a consequence of the replace- ment of unselective herbicides in IPint by mechanical weeding and selective herbicides in IPext.

Reduced impacts also resulted per kg of dry matter produced (Table 5(b)), although the differences were smaller and less signif- icant. Production conditions are favourable on this experimental site, with a soil rich in nutrients which allows the production of relatively high yields with a low nutrient input, leading to a yield loss of only 7% in IPext.

sity potential per area (Table 6(a)). The range of values is much lar- ger for grassland than for arable cropping systems. Arable crops - even if managed extensively - always need a minimum of inter- ventions for their cultivation (soil cultivation, seedbed preparation, sowing, harvest, etc.). In contrast, permanent grassland can be managed with only very little intervention; cutting once a year is sufficient to prevent repopulation by shrubs and trees. The main reasons for the reduction of environmental impacts in extensively managed grassland lie in fewer machinery operations (the main operations being the harvest processes) and in lower quantities of fertilisers. Since pesticides are applied in low quantities only, in contrast to arable crops they do not play an important role. The great increase in biodiversity potentials is an effect of less fre- quent cutting combined with the lower amount of nutrients sup- plied (no nutrients at all are given to the extensive meadow).

When analysing per product unit MJ net energy lactation (MJ NEL), the picture changes considerably (Table 6(b)). This functional unit takes into account the yield as well as the nutritional quality of the fodder and varies by almost a factor of six between the most intensive and the most extensive management (Table 2). As ex- pected, the differences were less marked than in the area-related analysis. For most of the environmental impact categories, the extensive meadow showed the most favourable results. Only for human toxicity potential did intensive management lead to a slightly lower impact. The surprising finding was that there was no clear gradient going from intensive to extensive management, but that the highest values were found for medium intensity man- agement: either low intensive (highest values for energy demand, global warming, aquatic ecotoxicity and human toxicity) or fairly intensive (highest values for eutrophication and terrestrial ecotox-

icity). Only for ozone formation did the intensive meadow show the highest impacts. We can see that in general medium intensity management was not preferable to intensive or extensive manage- ment in terms of product-related impacts; in other words extensi- fication has to be complete in order to achieve the desired effects for the environment.

Since by managing all grassland extensively it would not be possible to cover the quantitative and qualitative needs of the ani- mals, the combination of intensively and extensively managed plot is a reasonable compromise between environmental and economic goals. We calculated the combination "2/3Int + 1/3Ext", yielding the same amount of MJ NEL as the fairly intensive meadow, and the combination "3/4Int + 1/4Ext" which would give the same dry matter yield. These combinations showed a better environ- mental performance than fairly intensive management, with the exception of global warming and ozone formation. The low inten- sive management is interesting from the point of view of biodiver- sity, while the fairly intensive management hardly improved the biodiversity potential compared to intensive management.

icity). Only for ozone formation did the intensive meadow show the highest impacts. We can see that in general medium intensity management was not preferable to intensive or extensive manage- ment in terms of product-related impacts; in other words extensi- fication has to be complete in order to achieve the desired effects for the environment.

Since by managing all grassland extensively it would not be possible to cover the quantitative and qualitative needs of the ani- mals, the combination of intensively and extensively managed plot is a reasonable compromise between environmental and economic goals. We calculated the combination "2/3Int + 1/3Ext", yielding the same amount of MJ NEL as the fairly intensive meadow, and the combination "3/4Int + 1/4Ext" which would give the same dry matter yield. These combinations showed a better environ- mental performance than fairly intensive management, with the exception of global warming and ozone formation. The low inten- sive management is interesting from the point of view of biodiver- sity, while the fairly intensive management hardly improved the biodiversity potential compared to intensive management.

39: Applying smaller amounts of fertiliser reduced most environ- mental impacts per area unit (Table 7(a)). The differences were greatest in system C, which received the highest fertiliser input, and therefore experienced the greatest reduction in absolute terms. The main effects of reducing the intensity of fertiliser use are: saving fossil energy used for manufacturing mineral fertilisers in integrated and conventional agriculture, less machine opera- tions for spreading fertilisers, lower emissions of N (ammonia, ni- trate, nitrous oxide) and P, as well as reduced emissions of polluting substances such as heavy metals.

For soil quality we observed an adverse effect from the reduc- tion in fertiliser inputs. All these systems received slurry and man- ure and the reduction of the quantities thereof resulted in a negative humus balance with unfavourable effects on aggregate
stability, microbial biomass and activity. This highlights the impor- tance of ensuring a sufficient supply of organic matter to the soil.

Reducing the nutrient supply by 50% in the arable systems of the DOC trial hardly influenced the biodiversity potential; the model only estimated a clear rise in biodiversity potential in sys- tem DO without any fertiliser application. A greater reduction in nutrient input is required to obtain a significant improvement in biodiversity.

When the impacts were calculated per product unit (kg dry matter yield), the result pattern changed considerably (Table 7(b)). Due to the lower yields, the area needed to produce one unit increased when fertiliser use was reduced. C1 showed significantly lower impacts for global warming, P-resources, eutrophication and acidification, while the ecotoxicity and human toxicity poten- tials increased, albeit not significantly. The organic systems 01 and D1 had significantly lower global warming and acidification potentials than 02 and D2. As regards the unfertilised system D0, we estimated a significant increase of energy demand, ozone forma- tion, P-resources and human toxicity, while the values for global warming and acidification were still significantly lower. The reduc- tion of fertiliser use cannot be pushed too far without risking poor crop performance, and a minimum level of nutrient supply must be maintained not only for economic reasons, but also to ensure good eco-efficiency.

42: <header>3.4. Choice of fertiliser form: mineral and organic </header>

The impact of the form of fertiliser (mineral or organic) is inves- tigated in the DOC experiment and in a model study for grassland systems. Table 8 shows that switching from mixed fertiliser use in C2 (organic and mineral) to purely mineral fertiliser in M2 increased resource-driven impacts significantly (energy demand, ozone formation, P- and K-resource consumption), while it decreased acidification potential. The increased resource consumption was caused by mineral fertiliser manufacturing. The reductions in acidification and eutrophication were due to substantially lower

ammonia emissions from mineral fertilisers as compared to farmyard manure (mainly slurry and liquid manure). There was a lower risk of nitrate leaching as well, since mineral N can be given at the exact stage of vegetation when it is needed. Organically bound N, by contrast, needs to be mineralised first; a process that depends on weather and soil conditions and is difficult to control. The pollutant-driven impacts were not changed significantly. As far as the soil quality indicators are concerned, we observed a clearly negative effect for M2, similar to the no fertilisation plot DO. The biodiversity potential was not changed by switching the form of fertiliser.

In order to illustrate the environmental mechanisms, three dif- ferent fertiliser application regimes were studied for grassland sys- tems in a model study. All three systems received the same amount of available N, either in the form of mineral fertiliser, liquid man- ure or a combination of liquid and solid manure. Not surprisingly, energy demand was more than 50% diminished by the use of or- ganic fertilisers (Fig. 1). The energy required for machine opera- tions increased only slightly.
The global warming potential was not much changed (Fig. 2), there was a tendency for a slight decrease as already observed in the DOC experiment. However, the contribution of the different
greenhouse gases changed: CO2 and N20 were less important for organic fertilisers, while the contribution of CH4 (from manure storage) increased sharply.

Eutrophication was clearly higher with organic fertiliser use (Fig. 3) because of a 17-fold increase in ammonia emissions for li- quid manure as compared to mineral fertilisers and a rise in nitrate leaching as well, while other emissions like N20, P-compounds, and NO decreased. Due to lower emissions of ammonia from man- ure, the combination of liquid and solid manure turned out to be better.

ammonia emissions from mineral fertilisers as compared to farmyard manure (mainly slurry and liquid manure). There was a lower risk of nitrate leaching as well, since mineral N can be given at the exact stage of vegetation when it is needed. Organically bound N, by contrast, needs to be mineralised first; a process that depends on weather and soil conditions and is difficult to control. The pollutant-driven impacts were not changed significantly. As far as the soil quality indicators are concerned, we observed a clearly negative effect for M2, similar to the no fertilisation plot DO. The biodiversity potential was not changed by switching the form of fertiliser.

In order to illustrate the environmental mechanisms, three dif- ferent fertiliser application regimes were studied for grassland sys- tems in a model study. All three systems received the same amount of available N, either in the form of mineral fertiliser, liquid man- ure or a combination of liquid and solid manure. Not surprisingly, energy demand was more than 50% diminished by the use of or- ganic fertilisers (Fig. 1). The energy required for machine opera- tions increased only slightly.
The global warming potential was not much changed (Fig. 2), there was a tendency for a slight decrease as already observed in the DOC experiment. However, the contribution of the different
greenhouse gases changed: CO2 and N20 were less important for organic fertilisers, while the contribution of CH4 (from manure storage) increased sharply.

Eutrophication was clearly higher with organic fertiliser use (Fig. 3) because of a 17-fold increase in ammonia emissions for li- quid manure as compared to mineral fertilisers and a rise in nitrate leaching as well, while other emissions like N20, P-compounds, and NO decreased. Due to lower emissions of ammonia from man- ure, the combination of liquid and solid manure turned out to be better.

49: A special form of integrated production existing in Switzerland, the so-called "extensive production of cereals and rape seed" (DZV, 1998) was investigated. Farmers get additional direct payments if they dispense with fungicide, insecticide and growth regulator applications. The applications of herbicides and mineral fertilisers, however, are not restricted. This production form - although called "extensive production" - is in fact a form of extensive plant protec- tion. Under Swiss conditions the average yield is reduced by 17%, 21% and 14% for wheat, barley and rape seed respectively (Table 1).

In general in extensive production the amounts of herbicides are reduced as well (although the rules do not apply to them, see DZV, 1998) and fertiliser use is composed of a larger share of slurry and manure.

The results per hectare and year showed positive effects of extensive pesticide use for biodiversity, terrestrial ecotoxicity (bar- ley and rape seed), human toxicity for wheat and energy demand for rape seed (Table 9(a)). Many impacts were judged similar, but had slightly higher values. The impacts on soil quality were not analysed here, since the results should be interpreted in the con- text of crop rotation.

When it came to product-related impacts (Table 9(b)), extensive production turned out to be unfavourable in several cases (for glo- bal warming, ozone formation, eutrophication and acidification) and we obtained very favourable results only for terrestrial ecotoxicity.

The results of the evaluation per CHF return (Table 9(c)) lay in between the area- and the product-related evaluation for wheat and barley; in the case of rape seed it turned out to be even more favourable. The relative return from extensive compared to inten- sive production was 92%, 93% and 102% for wheat, barley and rape seed respectively, since the direct payments compensated partly or fully for the lower yields.

In summary we can conclude that the "extensive production" measure led to better results for biodiversity, ecotoxicity and hu- man toxicity, although the differences for toxicity were not always clear. This improvement was, however, at the expense of lower efficiency in the use of other resources like fossil energy and nutri- ents. This measure should therefore be improved, as already pointed out by Gaillard and Nemecek (2002).

As the last extensification option we evaluated the reduction of soil tillage, a key factor for machinery use in arable crops. The yields in the "Oberacker" experiment were 4% higher for the no-till treatment (Table 3); this treatment had also received slightly more mineral nitrogen (no organic fertiliser was applied in this experiment).

Favourable area-related impacts of the no-till procedure were found for global warming, human toxicity and soil quality (Table 10(a)). In fact all the impacts - except terrestrial ecotoxicity - turned out to be lower for the NT system and most of these dif- ferences were significant. The main reasons were marked savings in machinery operations with lower consumption of diesel and

In general in extensive production the amounts of herbicides are reduced as well (although the rules do not apply to them, see DZV, 1998) and fertiliser use is composed of a larger share of slurry and manure.

The results per hectare and year showed positive effects of extensive pesticide use for biodiversity, terrestrial ecotoxicity (bar- ley and rape seed), human toxicity for wheat and energy demand for rape seed (Table 9(a)). Many impacts were judged similar, but had slightly higher values. The impacts on soil quality were not analysed here, since the results should be interpreted in the con- text of crop rotation.

When it came to product-related impacts (Table 9(b)), extensive production turned out to be unfavourable in several cases (for glo- bal warming, ozone formation, eutrophication and acidification) and we obtained very favourable results only for terrestrial ecotoxicity.

The results of the evaluation per CHF return (Table 9(c)) lay in between the area- and the product-related evaluation for wheat and barley; in the case of rape seed it turned out to be even more favourable. The relative return from extensive compared to inten- sive production was 92%, 93% and 102% for wheat, barley and rape seed respectively, since the direct payments compensated partly or fully for the lower yields.

In summary we can conclude that the "extensive production" measure led to better results for biodiversity, ecotoxicity and hu- man toxicity, although the differences for toxicity were not always clear. This improvement was, however, at the expense of lower efficiency in the use of other resources like fossil energy and nutri- ents. This measure should therefore be improved, as already pointed out by Gaillard and Nemecek (2002).

As the last extensification option we evaluated the reduction of soil tillage, a key factor for machinery use in arable crops. The yields in the "Oberacker" experiment were 4% higher for the no-till treatment (Table 3); this treatment had also received slightly more mineral nitrogen (no organic fertiliser was applied in this experiment).

Favourable area-related impacts of the no-till procedure were found for global warming, human toxicity and soil quality (Table 10(a)). In fact all the impacts - except terrestrial ecotoxicity - turned out to be lower for the NT system and most of these dif- ferences were significant. The main reasons were marked savings in machinery operations with lower consumption of diesel and

lower tractor exhaust gas emissions, since ploughing is a very re- source-intensive operation. Furthermore, nitrate leaching was slightly reduced due to the abandonment of intensive soil cultiva- tion. Terrestrial ecotoxicity according to EDIP was classified as very unfavourable. The effect was due to the relatively frequent applica- tion of glyphosate in the NT system. This negative assessment, however, was not confirmed by the other two methods applied (CML and CST, see Nemecek et al., 2011). Glyphosate is quickly ad- sorbed to soil particles and is only occasionally leached to ground- water (Henkelmann, 2005; Vereecken, 2005). Phosphate is competing for the same sorption sites and the use of P fertiliser could therefore induce leaching of glyphosate (Vereecken, 2005). Although glyphosate is considered to be a less harmful active ingredient, we should restrict its application to the necessary minimum.
The effect of the no-till system on macropores and earthworm biomass turned out to be more favourable than the effect of

ploughing. Note that the SALCA soil quality method according to Oberholzer et al. (2006) was not particularly adapted to, or vali- dated for conditions of no-till and therefore probably underesti- mated the positive effects of this procedure. High erosion protection is a further positive effect of the no-till system; in the case of the experiment however, it could be shown only by model calculations, where a slope of 18% was assumed, since the experi- mental site has no slope. Due to the slightly higher yields in NT as compared to PL, the product-related impacts of NT were slightly more favourable than the area-related ones (Table 10(b)). This re- sulted in an even lower NT energy demand than for PL. In summary the no-till system was able to reduce the resource- driven impacts, to prevent soil erosion and to slightly reduce nitrate leaching. The most important positive effect was the conservation or improvement of soil quality. The application of glyphosate should be restricted in order to reduce risks to the envi- ronment and risks of resistance in weeds.

Impact|Unit|(a) Per ha and year|(a) Per ha and year|(b) Per DM yield|(b) Per DM yield Impact|Unit|Plough (PL)|No-till (NT)|Plough (PL)|No-till (NT) Energy demand|MJ-eq|16,050|14,746*|1655|1459* Global warming pot.|kg CO2-eq|2141|2070|221|205 Ozone formation|kg C2H4-eq|0.59|0.48*|0.061|0.048* Eutrophication|kg N-eq|48.4|45.2|4.99|4.47 Acidification|kg SO2-eq|14.1|13.2*|1.46|1.31* Aquatic ecotoxicity|points|1,006,119|840,749*|103,714|83,163* Terrestrial ecotoxicity|points|827,257|1,938,304*|85,276|191,728* Human toxicity|points|716|517*|73.8|51.2* Soil quality||||| Macropore volume||+|++|| Aggregate stability||+|+|| Earthworm biomass||+|++||

lower tractor exhaust gas emissions, since ploughing is a very re- source-intensive operation. Furthermore, nitrate leaching was slightly reduced due to the abandonment of intensive soil cultiva- tion. Terrestrial ecotoxicity according to EDIP was classified as very unfavourable. The effect was due to the relatively frequent applica- tion of glyphosate in the NT system. This negative assessment, however, was not confirmed by the other two methods applied (CML and CST, see Nemecek et al., 2011). Glyphosate is quickly ad- sorbed to soil particles and is only occasionally leached to ground- water (Henkelmann, 2005; Vereecken, 2005). Phosphate is competing for the same sorption sites and the use of P fertiliser could therefore induce leaching of glyphosate (Vereecken, 2005). Although glyphosate is considered to be a less harmful active ingredient, we should restrict its application to the necessary minimum.
The effect of the no-till system on macropores and earthworm biomass turned out to be more favourable than the effect of

ploughing. Note that the SALCA soil quality method according to Oberholzer et al. (2006) was not particularly adapted to, or vali- dated for conditions of no-till and therefore probably underesti- mated the positive effects of this procedure. High erosion protection is a further positive effect of the no-till system; in the case of the experiment however, it could be shown only by model calculations, where a slope of 18% was assumed, since the experi- mental site has no slope. Due to the slightly higher yields in NT as compared to PL, the product-related impacts of NT were slightly more favourable than the area-related ones (Table 10(b)). This re- sulted in an even lower NT energy demand than for PL. In summary the no-till system was able to reduce the resource- driven impacts, to prevent soil erosion and to slightly reduce nitrate leaching. The most important positive effect was the conservation or improvement of soil quality. The application of glyphosate should be restricted in order to reduce risks to the envi- ronment and risks of resistance in weeds.

lower tractor exhaust gas emissions, since ploughing is a very re- source-intensive operation. Furthermore, nitrate leaching was slightly reduced due to the abandonment of intensive soil cultiva- tion. Terrestrial ecotoxicity according to EDIP was classified as very unfavourable. The effect was due to the relatively frequent applica- tion of glyphosate in the NT system. This negative assessment, however, was not confirmed by the other two methods applied (CML and CST, see Nemecek et al., 2011). Glyphosate is quickly ad- sorbed to soil particles and is only occasionally leached to ground- water (Henkelmann, 2005; Vereecken, 2005). Phosphate is competing for the same sorption sites and the use of P fertiliser could therefore induce leaching of glyphosate (Vereecken, 2005). Although glyphosate is considered to be a less harmful active ingredient, we should restrict its application to the necessary minimum.
The effect of the no-till system on macropores and earthworm biomass turned out to be more favourable than the effect of

ploughing. Note that the SALCA soil quality method according to Oberholzer et al. (2006) was not particularly adapted to, or vali- dated for conditions of no-till and therefore probably underesti- mated the positive effects of this procedure. High erosion protection is a further positive effect of the no-till system; in the case of the experiment however, it could be shown only by model calculations, where a slope of 18% was assumed, since the experi- mental site has no slope. Due to the slightly higher yields in NT as compared to PL, the product-related impacts of NT were slightly more favourable than the area-related ones (Table 10(b)). This re- sulted in an even lower NT energy demand than for PL. In summary the no-till system was able to reduce the resource- driven impacts, to prevent soil erosion and to slightly reduce nitrate leaching. The most important positive effect was the conservation or improvement of soil quality. The application of glyphosate should be restricted in order to reduce risks to the envi- ronment and risks of resistance in weeds.
62: Do extensive production or low-input farming systems offer a way towards more sustainable production from an environmental point of view? In this paper we have studied two solutions for the extensification of Swiss arable farming and forage production sys- tems: (1) overall reduction of intensity and (2) partial reduction of intensity.
63: The overall extensification in the farming system experiment at Burgrain seems to be a successful one. Most of the environmental impacts have been reduced, many of them significantly. The reduc- tion was observed in the area-related as well as the product- related analysis. Although the differences were not very large in many cases, implementing such a farming system on a large scale offers considerable environmental improvement potential. As inte- grated production covers 90% of the Swiss agricultural area, an improvement in the latter can reduce environmental impacts significantly. The only disadvantage of the extensive system at Burgrain was the slight decrease in some soil quality indicators, which can be explained by the lower quantities of organic fertiliser and their different modes of application. The analysis of the eco- nomic performance of these farming systems has shown that the gross margins were slightly lower for potato, grain maize and spring barley, but slightly increased for winter wheat (Tschachtli et al., 2004); the overall differences were only small. In the system the lower direct costs and the increased direct payment could not fully compensate for the lower yields.

The extensification of grassland management resulted in a dif- ferent picture: while extensive management led to the best envi- ronmental performance, both per hectare and per MJ NEL, medium intensity management did not reduce all the environmen- tal impacts per product unit; on the contrary, many impacts even increased. The reason for these differences in eco-efficiency lies in the complex interactions of the inputs applied and the quality and quantity of the product harvested. On the input side, the machinery operations and related inputs (diesel, machinery infra- structure, and tractor exhaust gases) were reduced due to a lower number of cuts. The required machine operations per cut were as- sumed to be independent of the yield with the exception of baling, which is directly dependent on the quantity of hay. On the output side, yields decreased with the lower number of cuts, but the en- ergy yield per cut was not constant: it decreased in the following order: intensive (12.8 NEL/cut), fairly intensive (11.7 NEL/ cut), extensive (11.3 GJ NEL/cut), and low intensive (8.9 GJ cut). The latter value was 31% lower than the energy yield of the intensive meadow, which explains the poor efficiency of the machinery use in low intensive management. The input of avail- able nitrogen decreased as well, with lower intensity. Expressed per product unit it was quite similar for the intensive and the fairly intensive systems, substantially lower for the low intensive system and zero for extensive management (Table 2). The behaviour shown by the nutrient-driven impacts was therefore different to that of the resource-driven impacts. Note that in grassland man- agement - unlike in arable crops - pesticides play only a minor role, even in ecotoxicity and human toxicity. Emissions related to machinery operations were therefore largely responsible for these impacts. When it came to biodiversity, fairly intensive manage- ment brought almost no advantage over intensive management, while the low intensive system and even more extensive manage- ment resulted in significantly higher biodiversity values. In order to increase the biodiversity potential, the low intensive system could be a good alternative to fairly intensive production. For all

other environmental impacts extensive management clearly out- performed low intensive management, so that the latter cannot be generally recommended. As shown in Table 6, a combination of intensive and extensive gave better overall results and allowed the required fodder quality to be produced as well. Hay from extensive meadows is of poor quality only and is therefore suited only to a few feeding applications.

Intensity and environmental impacts were much more variable for grassland management than for arable crops (see results for the "Burgrain", "Oberacker" and DOC experiments as well as those for the model arable crops). The reason lies in the nature of arable crops and grassland respectively: while we need several interven- tions to grow arable crops (soil cultivation, sowing, harvest, and minimum care), the activities necessary to maintain grassland can be kept at a very low level (basically one cut per year to pre- vent reforestation of the area).

66: The LCA results from the DOC experiment suggest that halving the fertiliser doses in conventional production (C2 C1) signifi- cantly reduces all the environmental impacts per ha (Table 7(a)), except ecotoxicity and human toxicity, which depended almost en- tirely on pesticide treatments and remained constant. The biodi- versity potential was hardly affected. Analysed per kg of product (Table 7(b)), global warming, acidification and eutrophication were significantly lessened, while area occupation increased due to the lower yields. We observed a non-significant increase in ecotoxicity and human toxicity, which can be explained by the lower yields. From an environmental point of view the lower fertiliser doses were preferable; however, care must be taken to provide the soil with sufficient organic matter in order to maintain soil quality in the long run.

These findings are consistent with results of Brentrup et al. (2004), who found that approximately 100 kg N/ha, about half the amount currently applied, would be environmentally optimal for winter wheat in England. He also showed that the environmen- tal impacts per kg of wheat grain increase if fertiliser use falls be- low an optimum level. A similar trend was also observed for the DOC experiment: the no fertiliser procedure DO showed higher im- pacts in most environmental categories. Only for global warming, acidification and biodiversity were the results more favourable. Interestingly, the eutrophication potential per kg of product was the same as that of D2. Nutrient losses were drastically reduced thanks to the absence of fertiliser use, but the yield fell to the same extent.

The advantages of lower fertiliser use were much less visible in the organic farming systems D and o; it seems that at the current level of fertiliser application (D2 and 02, with 1.2-1.4 live- stock units/ha) the organic systems were quite close to the ecolog- ical optimum. The reduction of nutrient input was exactly 50% in all systems from level 2 to level 1; in absolute terms however, this diminution was much lower in the organic systems receiving less nutrients than the conventional ones (Nemecek et al., 2011).

Replacing mineral fertiliser by farmyard manure is another way to reduce farm-external inputs. The main advantages were found to be the saving of resources (fossil fuels and mineral resources of P and K) and an increase or better maintenance of soil quality. The main drawbacks were the higher nutrient losses, since organic fertilisers are more difficult to manage and to control (Gaillard and Nemecek, 2006).

The introduction of legumes is a further option for reducing the use of fertilisers. Nemecek et al. (2008) showed that the introduc- tion of grain legumes into cereal rich crop rotations could reduce energy demand, global warming, ozone formation, acidification

and ecotoxicity in intensive crop rotations with a high N input. Le- gumes enable farming systems to be less dependent on external non-renewable resources. Care must be taken to effectively control nitrate leaching by introducing catch crops, intercrops or by sowing winter grain legumes. Grass-legume mixtures rich in legumes are another way to reduce several environmental impacts (Nemecek et al., 2005b).

70: It is important to use techniques reducing the number of pesti- cide treatments and the amounts of active matter, in order to mit- igate the adverse side-effects of pesticides on ecosystems. The use of pesticides is still very high in Europe (EUROSTAT, 2007); in Switzerland it was possible to reduce the quantities of pesticide, mainly thanks to the introduction of cross compliance in the 1990s (Flury, 2005).

There are several possible ways of reducing the amount of pes- ticide. Among them are the use of resistant varieties, crop and cul- tivar mixtures, the use of disease and pest forecasting systems and damage thresholds, the replacement of herbicides by mechanical weeding, split and strip treatments, optimised application tech- niques, precision farming and the use of biological or physical con- trol measures. Care must be taken that treatment reduction does not provoke a substantial loss in product yield or quality. The examples presented in this paper show that a ban on certain pes- ticide categories (fungicides, insecticides and growth regulators in the case of extensive production) is able to reduce ecotoxicity and human toxicity and to increase biodiversity, but this result is achieved at the expense of lower resource efficiency. This means that more fossil fuels and more fertilisers are used to produce the same quantity of product. In respect of lower yield expectation it should also be possible to reduce the input of fertilisers. In real- ity, however, this does not happen; on the contrary, the trend dur- ing recent years has been to increase the nitrogen input in extensive wheat, leading to a positive N balance, as shown recently in a pilot farm network (Favre et al., 2006).

Several LCA studies have shown that only certain active ingredi- ents were responsible for most of the ecotoxicity potential. There- fore not only a reduction in pesticide quantity is important. A substantial reduction in ecotoxicity can be achieved by replacing one critical active ingredient with another (see e.g. Gaillard and Nemecek, 2002).

The example of the extensive production of cereals and rape seed shows that selective extensification by banning certain clas- ses of pesticide may not result in an overall environmental improvement. It is important to consider all the elements of a farming system.
71: It is important to use techniques reducing the number of pesti- cide treatments and the amounts of active matter, in order to mit- igate the adverse side-effects of pesticides on ecosystems. The use of pesticides is still very high in Europe (EUROSTAT, 2007); in Switzerland it was possible to reduce the quantities of pesticide, mainly thanks to the introduction of cross compliance in the 1990s (Flury, 2005).

There are several possible ways of reducing the amount of pes- ticide. Among them are the use of resistant varieties, crop and cul- tivar mixtures, the use of disease and pest forecasting systems and damage thresholds, the replacement of herbicides by mechanical weeding, split and strip treatments, optimised application tech- niques, precision farming and the use of biological or physical con- trol measures. Care must be taken that treatment reduction does not provoke a substantial loss in product yield or quality. The examples presented in this paper show that a ban on certain pes- ticide categories (fungicides, insecticides and growth regulators in the case of extensive production) is able to reduce ecotoxicity and human toxicity and to increase biodiversity, but this result is achieved at the expense of lower resource efficiency. This means that more fossil fuels and more fertilisers are used to produce the same quantity of product. In respect of lower yield expectation it should also be possible to reduce the input of fertilisers. In real- ity, however, this does not happen; on the contrary, the trend dur- ing recent years has been to increase the nitrogen input in extensive wheat, leading to a positive N balance, as shown recently in a pilot farm network (Favre et al., 2006).

Several LCA studies have shown that only certain active ingredi- ents were responsible for most of the ecotoxicity potential. There- fore not only a reduction in pesticide quantity is important. A substantial reduction in ecotoxicity can be achieved by replacing one critical active ingredient with another (see e.g. Gaillard and Nemecek, 2002).

The example of the extensive production of cereals and rape seed shows that selective extensification by banning certain clas- ses of pesticide may not result in an overall environmental improvement. It is important to consider all the elements of a farming system.

73: The third extensification option evaluated is a reduction in soil tillage intensity. Soil cultivation by means of plough or other con- verting and mixing tools are among the deepest agricultural inter- ventions in terrestrial ecosystems, since the soil plays a key role.

As the example of the LCA in the "Oberacker" experiment showed, the no-till system achieved a better environmental perfor- mance than the cropping system using a plough. The main advan- tages of this farming system were resource conservation, the prevention of water pollution by reducing erosion, run-off and leaching, and the preservation or increase of soil quality (Holland, 2004; Schubetzer et al., 2007). It is particularly to be recommended in sites with a high risk of erosion (steep slopes, susceptible soils, heavy rainfall), since erosion control is quite efficient in conserva- tion tillage. No-tillage farming is considered as a way to increase soil organic carbon and thus to contribute to carbon sequestration (Holland, 2004), but this has been recently called into question by Baker et al. (2007), who claimed that conservation tillage leads

merely to a redistribution of the carbon in the soil. In a recent re- view Schubetzer et al. (2007) also concluded that the potential of carbon sequestration due to conservation tillage is quite limited.

The no-till system is not suited to all crops and cropping sys- items. E.g. potato has proved to be inadequate for this kind of farm- ing in the given site and was therefore removed from the experiment after the first crop rotation (Chervet et al., 2005).

Although soil cultivation is at the centre of these farming sys- tems and therefore they are treated as partial extensification in this paper, the change in the farming system is quite fundamental and goes beyond soil cultivation only; in fact plant protection (e.g. weed control, control of fungal diseases on crop residues) and fer- tiliser application have to be adapted in no-till agriculture as well.

75: <header>4.3. Eco-efficiency of intensive and extensive farming </header>

Multi-functional agriculture strives for several goals: it has (i) to provide a sufficient amount of products for use as food, feed, renewable energy and materials, (ii) to conserve natural resources such as soil, biodiversity and landscape and (iii) to establish a basis for a rural population (LwG, 1998). These goals have inherent con- flicts and cannot be fully achieved in the same system. Viglizzo and Roberto (1998) discussed the trade-offs between productivity, sta- bility and sustainability in low-input farming systems. They found that higher productivity is at the expense of the stability and sus- tainability of the system, but that there are important exceptions relevant to low-input farming systems. Notably, increased diver- sity and complexity of the agro-ecosystem increased its stability and sustainability. Such higher diversity can be achieved e.g. by moving from monoculture to diversified crop rotations or by intro- ducing new trophic levels (e.g. turning a pure cash crop farm into a mixed farm).
For the sustainable development of agriculture we have to look at the relationship between the different functions of agriculture and the inherent conflicts in achieving the goals.

We can conclude from the results presented for organic and integrated production (Nemecek et al., 2011) as well as those for intensive and extensive farming paper) that the environmen- tal impacts per area unit (land management function) are mini- mised in organic and low-input farming respectively. The lower the level of input and management intensity, the lower the envi- ronmental impacts per hectare. Every input is normally related to some resource use and some adverse impacts on the environ- ment, therefore a reduction in inputs or interventions reduces area-related environmental impacts, with only very few exceptions.

The result is different if the productive function, i.e. the prod- uct-related environmental impact, is the focus of interest. The out- come then depends on the yield response to an additional unit of input on the one hand and to the environmental impact provoked by this additional impact. Tilman et al. (2002) claimed that the sus- tainable provisioning of food for a growing world population calls for a marked increase in the efficiency of the use of resources and inputs (nutrients, water, soil, pesticides). Input use efficiency is im- proved if the relative yield increase is higher than the relative in- crease of the environmental impact (Nemecek et al., 2005a). The objective is to find inputs and techniques with a highly positive yield effect and low environmental burdens at the same time. The environmental optimum depends on the shape of the yield re- sponse and the environmental impact curves as a function of input level. In some systems the most extensive management gives the lowest impact per product unit, while in others intensive manage- ment is favourable. In many situations the environmental opti- mum is found at medium intensity, while in some cases medium intensity is unfavourable and either high or low intensity or a com- bination of both gives the best results. Green et al. (2005) showed

in the case of nature conservation and biodiversity that in certain situations intensive management leaving space free for extensive areas is preferable, while in other cases extensive management of the whole area is more environmentally friendly. The environ- mental optimum cannot be predicted without a detailed knowl- edge of and good data on a farming system and its behaviour. Income-related analysis again showed a different pattern to the area- and the product-related evaluations. The analysis per unit of return could not be done for all systems due to lack of data. The available results (Nemecek et al., 2005b, 2008) show a tendency for the income-related evaluation to resemble the area-related more than the product-related function. There are several reasons for this: (i) In low-input systems the input costs are often reduced, so that part of the income loss due to lower yield can be compen- sated for. (ii) Products from extensive production, organic products in particular, realise higher prices on the market. (iii) Area-related direct payments in a cross-compliance scheme compensate for part of the yield losses.

79: The results presented show a general tendency for extensive production to lead to lower environmental impacts, both per area unit and per product unit. It is important to observe the interrela- tionships between the different elements of a farming system in order to keep a good overall balance. Changes in the level of one input may imply changes in other inputs. The farming system should be considered in its entirety. In arable cropping systems medium production intensity seems to give the best results for the environment, and the intensity should not fall below a certain level; otherwise a deterioration of eco-efficiency will be the conse- quence. In grassland systems, on the contrary, medium manage- ment intensity gave worse results than extensive or intensive management. A combination of both intensively and extensively managed plots seems to be the most promising solution. The dif- ferences in yield, production intensity and environmental impact were much more pronounced in grassland than in arable cropping systems.

Partial extensification of a farming system can also be environ- mentally favourable, but must be seen in the context of the whole system. An extensification of fertiliser use and soil cultivation alone resulted in improved environmental performance of the farming system, whereas a reduction of plant protection intensity by banning certain pesticide categories reduced negative impacts on ecotoxicity and biodiversity, while increasing other burdens such as global warming, ozone formation, eutrophication and acid- ification per product unit.

These results show that a considerable environmental improve- ment potential exists in Swiss farming systems and that a detailed eco-efficiency analysis could help in reducing their environmental impacts.
